2018.1: Leaving Paris behind, Tom and Julien headed north through the suburban sprawl of the Île-de-France, trading city streets for the first open roads of the challenge. At 27km with 203m of climbing, the legs were fresh and the adventure was just beginning — a perfect opening act for what lay ahead.
2018.2: The second day pushed deeper into the Oise countryside, rolling farmland opening up on either side as the two runners settled into their rhythm. Nearly 28km at a steady pace, with the kind of easy miles that make you forget how far you still have to go.
2018.3: The longest day yet — almost 30km with 287m of elevation — as the route wound through the hills north of Compiègne. The climb in the legs was starting to accumulate, but so was the satisfying knowledge that they were genuinely moving across France on foot.
2018.4: A hard day through the Aisne valley, 27km with 278m of gain that kept the effort honest. The countryside was beautiful in that quiet, northern French way — wide skies, bare fields, and the odd village church marking the kilometres.
2018.5: Wednesday brought the biggest test of the week: nearly 30km and 253m of climbing as the pair crossed into the Somme. The body starts to negotiate with you around day five — Tom and Julien told it no.
2018.6: A welcome flattening of the terrain as the route dropped toward the Pas-de-Calais plain, 29.5km with just 121m of elevation. The legs had found their suffering groove by now, and Lille was beginning to feel real.
2018.7: The shortest day of the challenge — 23km — but after six consecutive days of running, every kilometre still had to be earned. The finish line was close enough to smell, and the pace reflected it.
2018.8: The final stretch into Lille: 27km across the flat northern plain, every step a small celebration. After eight days and 220km on foot from Paris, Tom and Julien ran into the city together — the first edition complete, the tradition born.

2021.1: Setting off from Aachen in autumn light, the first day of the second edition covered 25km through the rolling hills of the Belgian-German border country. A different landscape to 2018, a different season — the same two lunatics heading west toward Lille.
2021.2: The route descended from the Ardennes foothills into the Meuse valley, 25km with 230m of climbing that kept the opening days honest. Three years between editions had done nothing to dull the appetite for this particular brand of self-inflicted adventure.
2021.3: A 27km day through the Liège hinterland, the industrial heart of Belgium visible in the distance before the route curved back into farmland. The elevation was gentler now, but the cumulative fatigue was starting its quiet work.
2021.4: The hardest day of the 2021 edition: 27km with 326m of climbing through the Brabant Wallon. The hills between Namur and Brussels don't announce themselves — they just keep coming, one after another, until the legs are done arguing.
2021.5: Another 27km day, this time with 208m of gain as the route pressed westward through the heart of Belgium. The language changed from French to Dutch and back again — welcome distraction from the accumulating kilometres in the legs.
2021.6: 26km through the flat Hainaut countryside, the terrain finally relenting after the climbs of earlier days. Lille was now close enough to navigate toward instinctively, the end of the tunnel visible.
2021.7: The penultimate day — 27km through the Franco-Belgian border country, retracing in reverse the history of a region that has seen armies march across it for centuries. Two runners made considerably less noise.
2021.8: The longest day of the 2021 edition: 31km to bring it home into Lille. No blog post was written — some finishes speak for themselves. Aachen to Lille, done.

2026.1: Out of London on a day that already felt like summer — 32km from the city into the Kent countryside, 466m of climbing through the North Downs, and a heat that had no business being this intense in May. The adventure was beginning; so was the suffering.
2026.2: Day two pushed east through the Weald, 32km and 456m of elevation under a sky that offered no mercy. The Kent hills are deceptively relentless — every descent promises a flat road that never quite arrives.
2026.3: Into Maidstone and beyond, 29km with 319m of climbing through orchard country baking in the unprecedented heat. The rhythm was there, the legs were holding — the sun, however, was not playing fair.
2026.4: The day everything changed. Ashford to the coast: 35.5km, 667m of climbing, 4 hours and 37 minutes under a vertical sun — the hardest stats of the entire challenge across all three editions. Somewhere on that road Tom fell hard, probable broken rib, and filed the pain away for later. He kept running. Then ran four more days.
2026.5: Crossing the Channel behind them, the route entered French territory for the first time — 29km through the Pas-de-Calais with 352m of gain. The landscape shifted, the language shifted, and the finish line in Lille began its gravitational pull.
2026.6: A flatter day at last, 31km with just 186m of elevation as the route moved through the bocage country of the Boulonnais. The body was deep in its damage-management phase; the mind had long since taken over.
2026.7: 34km with 466m of climbing through the hills of Artois — another brutal day this deep into the challenge. Every metre of elevation at this point requires a negotiation with muscles that have stopped believing in tomorrow.
2026.8: The final day: a flat 33km across the plain of Flandres into Lille. After 256km on foot from London, through a historic heat wave, through a fall that should have ended everything — Tom and Julien ran into Lille together. Later, it turns out, is now.
